m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is used to keep the call by reference value as intact?
m_Item1.Explanation: const keyword is use to keep value intact.when any varibale is declare with const keyword then value of that varible is regid throught the program.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which is referred by pointers to member?
m_Item1.Explanation: Pointer is holding address of non-static member of class object.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
 void Values(int n1, int n2 = 10)   {
    cout << "1st value: '"<< n1;
    cout << "2nd value: " << n2;
}
    int main()  {
        Values(1);
        Values(3, 4);
        return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: A default argument is a default value provided for a function parameter. If the user does not supply an explicit argument for a parameter with a default argument, the default value will be used. If the user does supply an argument for the parameter, the user-supplied argument is used.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Non-default arguments may have___________
m_Item1.Explanation: Default parameter fucntion is facility where you can call function without passing all it's  parameter.If function has no default argument must have arguments to call function.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int func(int m = 10, int n) {   
        int c;
        c = m + n;
        return c;
}
int main()     {
        cout << func(5);
        return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: Default argument must be from righthand side. Trailing arguments.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
void print(int i)   {
     cout << i;
}
 void print(double  f) {
     cout << f;
}
int main(void)
{
     print(5);
     print(500.263);
     return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
1.Function Overloading:
There are two overloaded functions print: one takes an int argument and the other takes a double argument.
2.Function Calls in main:
print(5); calls the print function with an int argument. This invokes void print(int i), which prints the integer 5.
print(500.263); calls the print function with a double argument. This invokes void print(double f), which prints the double 500.263.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of this program?
#include <iostream>
using namespace std;
int Add(int X, int Y, int Z) {
    return X + Y;
}
double Add(double X, double Y, double Z)  {
    return X + Y;
}
int main() {
     cout << Add(5, 6);
     cout << Add(5.5, 6.6);
     return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation: can not call function with two argument

m_Item1.QuestionText: Whenever const objects try to invoke non-const member functions, the compiler …………………
m_Item1.Explanation: Const object can invoke only const member fucntion. If you try to invoke non-const fucntion with const object compiler  gives compile time error.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Identify the incorrect statement.

Options.OptionText: Reference is the alternate name of the object
Rereference value once defined can be reassigned
Rereference value once defined cannot be reassigned
Reference is the alternate name of the variable

m_Item1.Explanation: Once initialized, a reference can not be changed to reference another variable.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream.h>
void main()
{
char p[]="This is a test";
cout<<sizeof(p)<<","<<strlen(p);
}
m_Item1.Explanation: p is character array without size. But when we initilized array , size of array is optional. compiler count element and decide size, so size of array is 15 . Number of actual element and one more for  '\0'  last character of string.and string length is actual elements from array that is 14. So answer is B.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
void main()
{
char str1[]=” India” , str2[]=” India” ;
if(str1==str2)
cout<<”Both the string are same” ;
else
cout<<”Both the string are not same” ;
}

Options.OptionText: Both the string are not same

m_Item1.Explanation:
The condition str1 == str2 evaluates to false because str1 and str2 are different arrays stored at different memory locations.
Name of array is base address of array. So str1 and str2 these are addresses. We can not compare strings with addresses , strings can be compare with built in fucntion strcmp();

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true regarding function overloading ?

Options.OptionText: Signature should be same, but return type should be different
Signature should be same and return type also should be same
Signature should be different and return type also should be different
Signature should be different and return type does not matter

m_Item1.Explanation: With function overloading, multiple functions can have the same name with different parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Default parameter can be placed by the user  on _____________ .
m_Item1.Explanation: If multiple parameters are provided as default ,In this case default parameter is placed from rightmost side.

m_Item1.QuestionText:
What is the output of the following code?
#include<iostream>
using namespace std;
int main() {
for(int ii=0;ii<3;++ii)
{
switch(ii)
{
case 0:cout<<"zero ";
case 1:cout<<"one ";continue;
case 2:cout<<"two ";break;
}
}
return 0;
}
m_Item1.Explanation:
Continue keyword is use here in switch case but because switch is inside fop loop  so it execute as follows .
When ii is 0 it will print Zero and because break is not used it will print one
When ii is 1 it goes to case 1 and print one
When ii is 2 it goes to case 2 and print two

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operation is supported by pointer variable in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: pointer arithamic allows us to add ,subtract and to assign value of one pointer to another pointer pointer .

m_Item1.QuestionText: C++ was originally called
m_Item1.Explanation: C++ was initially known as “C with classes, ” and was renamed C++ in 1983. ++ is shorthand for adding one to variety in programming; therefore C++ roughly means that “one higher than C.”

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which C++ oops feature is related to re-usability?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inheritance id 'is a' relationship which leads to re-usability.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following permits function overloading on c++ ?
m_Item1.Explanation: Function overloading is a feature in C++ where two or more functions can have the same name but different signiture.Signiture cantains data type ,number of argument and sequence of argumant.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is function overloading in C++?

Options.OptionText: Using the same function name with different return types
Using the same function name with different parameter types
Using the same function name in different classes
Using different function names with the same parameters

m_Item1.Explanation: Function overloading in C++ allows the same function name to be used for different functions with different parameter types or numbers.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is an example of valid function overloading?

Options.OptionText: void func(int, double); void func(double, int);

m_Item1.Explanation: Function overloading allows the use of the same function name with different parameters (number or types), hence all the given options are valid examples of function overloading.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if two functions differ only in return type can be overloaded in C++?

Options.OptionText: It is allowed and functions are overloaded

m_Item1.Explanation: In C++, functions cannot be overloaded by return type alone. The parameter list must differ in order to overload functions.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword can be used in C++ to distinguish overloaded functions in a class?
m_Item1.Explanation: No special keyword is needed to distinguish overloaded functions in a class. Overloading is resolved based on function signatures.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Consider two overloaded functions: foo(int a, double b) and foo(double a, int b). Which of the following calls is ambiguous?
m_Item1.Explanation: Calling foo(10, 20) results in ambiguity because both function signatures can be matched by converting either of the parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following statements about function overloading and default arguments is true?

Options.OptionText: Default arguments can be used to resolve overloaded functions.
Overloaded functions cannot have default arguments.
Default arguments do not affect function overloading.
If two functions differ only by default arguments, they cannot be overloaded.

m_Item1.Explanation: If two functions differ only by their default arguments, they cannot be considered overloaded, as default arguments do not contribute to function signatures for overloading.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Given three functions: foo(int), foo(double), and foo(float), which function is called with foo(5.0)?
m_Item1.Explanation: The function foo(double) is called because 5.0 is a double literal. C++ will select the best match based on the type of the argument.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Overloading can be applied to which of the following?

Options.OptionText: All functions, including member and non-member functions

m_Item1.Explanation: Overloading can be applied to any function, whether it is a member or non-member function, including particular overloading for constructors.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which operator cannot be overloaded in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The conditional operator (?:) cannot be overloaded in C++. Operators like +, =, and * can be overloaded.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What distinguishes overloaded functions in C++?

Options.OptionText: They must have different return types
They must belong to the same class
They must have different numbers or types of parameters
They must have different exception specifications

m_Item1.Explanation: Overloaded functions in C++ are distinguished by having a different number or types of parameters.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true about a const variable in C++?

Options.OptionText: Its value can be changed after initialization.
It must be initialized at the time of declaration.
It can be assigned a value later in the program.
It can be initialized in a function body.

m_Item1.Explanation: A const variable in C++ must be initialized at the time of declaration. Once initialized, its value cannot be changed.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the result of attempting to modify a const variable in C++?

Options.OptionText: The program will crash at runtime.
The value will change without any error.
The operation will be ignored silently.

m_Item1.Explanation: Attempting to modify a const variable in C++ will result in a compile-time error as its value is immutable.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which keyword is used to define a constant member function in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The keyword 'const' is used to define a constant member function in C++. This indicates that the function will not modify any class members.

m_Item1.QuestionText: In C++, what does a constant member function ensure?

Options.OptionText: It can modify the object it is called on.
It cannot be called by non-const objects.
It does not modify any member variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: A constant member function in C++ ensures that it does not modify any member variables or call any non-const member functions of the class.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where can the 'const' qualifier be applied in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: The 'const' qualifier can be applied to both variables and functions in C++. It makes variables immutable and ensures that functions do not modify class members.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if a non-const member function is called on a const object in C++?

Options.OptionText: The program will crash at runtime.
The call will succeed without errors.
A compiler warning will be generated.

m_Item1.Explanation: If a non-const member function is called on a const object, a compile-time error will occur because the function might modify that object.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following is true when passing a parameter as a const reference in C++?

Options.OptionText: The parameter can be modified within the function.
A new copy of the parameter is made.
The parameter cannot be modified within the function.

m_Item1.Explanation: Passing a parameter as a const reference in C++ means that the function can read but not modify the parameter.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the purpose of the 'mutable' keyword in C++ in the context of 'const'?

Options.OptionText: It makes a class member constant.
It allows a const member function to modify a member variable.
It prevents a variable from being overridden.
It is used to prevent inheritance.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'mutable' keyword allows a specific member variable to be modified even in a const member function, bypassing the const constraint.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which code correctly declares a constant pointer to an int in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: 'int* const ptr;' declares a constant pointer to an int, meaning the address stored in the pointer cannot change, but the value at that address can.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What does 'const_cast' allow you to do in C++?

Options.OptionText: Cast away the constness of variables.

m_Item1.Explanation: The 'const_cast' allows you to cast away the constness of variables in C++. It is used to remove or add the const modifier.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is the main advantage of using an inline function in C++?

Options.OptionText: It reduces the size of the binary.
It avoids the function call overhead.

m_Item1.Explanation: Inline functions are used to eliminate the overhead of a function call by inserting the function's code at the point where the function is called.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which of the following scenarios is a suitable candidate for an inline function?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inline functions are best suited for small functions that are frequently called, to optimize execution speed by reducing call overhead.

m_Item1.QuestionText: How does the `inline` keyword affect the compiler?

Options.OptionText: It mandates that the function be inlined.
It suggests to the compiler to attempt inlining.
It restricts the function from being duplicated.

m_Item1.Explanation: The `inline` keyword acts as a request to the compiler to inline the function, though the compiler may choose to ignore it.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Where should an inline function ideally be defined?
m_Item1.Explanation: Inline functions should be defined in header files so that their code can be included wherever they are called, ensuring inlining is possible.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What is a potential drawback of using too many inline functions?

Options.OptionText: Increased compile time and code bloat.

m_Item1.Explanation: Excessive use of inline functions can lead to increased binary size and longer compile times, often referred to as code bloat.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What will happen if an inline function definition is larger than what the compiler considers appropriate for inlining?

Options.OptionText: The compiler will still inline it.
The compiler will ignore the inline request.

m_Item1.Explanation: If the function is too large, the compiler may choose to ignore the inline suggestion and treat the function as a regular function, leaving inlining out.

m_Item1.QuestionText: When is it guaranteed that a function is an inline function during execution?

Options.OptionText: When it is defined in the same file.
When it uses the inline attribute.
It is never guaranteed due to compiler discretion.
When it only contains a return statement.

m_Item1.Explanation: Inlining is not guaranteed because it ultimately depends on the compiler's optimization processes and heuristics.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What type of functions can explicitly not be made inline in C++?
m_Item1.Explanation: Virtual functions are resolved through a virtual table at runtime, thus they cannot be inlined.

m_Item1.QuestionText: What happens if an inline function is defined in multiple source files?

Options.OptionText: The linker will issue a duplicate symbol error.
Each file will have its own copy of the function.

m_Item1.Explanation: Each source file that includes the inline function will have its own copy, as inline functions have external linkage.

m_Item1.QuestionText: Which statement is true about inline functions and macros?

Options.OptionText: Inline functions can cause side effects.
Macros are type safe while inline functions are not.
Both inline functions and macros are expanded by the preprocessor.

m_Item1.Explanation: Inline functions ensure type safety, whereas macros do not perform type checking and can easily cause side effects.
